notes:
The interlude shelf — what the summer prints onto the player's bookshelf once they've made the choices that earn each one. Per _COMMUNITY_PLANNED_SPEC.md §The interlude economy: the player closes the summer with a small extra book about the shoulder-season, written into existence by a hundred quiet turns. The novels are sovereign; these interludes never rewrite canon. They deepen.

Earn conditions are predicates evaluated at the end of each ADVANCE DAY against the run's accumulated state. Once an interlude is earned, it goes onto the shelf permanently and cannot be lost. A given run earns 8-14 of these depending on play style.

small_wood_seed_interludes.title: The Room Above the Office Holds
small_wood_seed_interludes.flavor: Labor Day, late Sunday, 7:42 PM Pacific. The room above the timber yard is still rented in Frasier's name. The light through the window is the colour the light through that window is in the first week of September: brass into copper into the bluest grey. Jules locks the office at 8:00. He walks home the long way. He does not, this evening, stop on the bridge.

small_wood_seed_interludes.title: The Dirt Road Took
small_wood_seed_interludes.flavor: The dirt road north of the timber yard had been a target since June. By early August Frasier's network had bent it twice — a quiet contact at the surveyor's office, a missed appointment with the press, the right hand on the right shoulder at a Wednesday town hall. By the morning of August 31 it was a held node. Jules drove it on a Sunday afternoon. There was nobody on it. The pines on the east side of it were the pines they had always been. The road did not know it had been planted; the road was, this fall, the road.

small_wood_seed_interludes.title: The Diner That Doesn't Know It's a Node Yet
small_wood_seed_interludes.flavor: The diner is on the highway, three miles north of town. The cook is sixty-four. The waitress is his niece. By August they were comping Jules's coffee on Tuesdays without asking. By September the cook had started asking, when Jules came in, who was meeting him today. Jules said, every time: nobody. The cook nodded every time. He never asked again. The diner became, by the end of September, the kind of node that you cannot tell a person about because telling a person about it would un-make it. Frasier's ledger has a single line for it: the diner held.

small_wood_seed_interludes.title: The Seed That Didn't
small_wood_seed_interludes.flavor: There was a dirt road north of the timber yard that did not, this summer, take. Frasier sent Vagrant in May; Vagrant got found at the local press. He sent Filly in June; Filly was intercepted at the state line and the letter she was carrying ran on page B6 of the Small Wood Gazette under a fake byline. He sent Cicada in July; Cicada surfaced at the wrong town meeting and Phase Three lost the cul-de-sac for a week as a consequence. By August he stopped sending. The road is a road. It is, now and for the next several summers, not on the map.

small_wood_seed_interludes.title: Jules Stayed With It
small_wood_seed_interludes.flavor: Eight years before this summer Jules Hemming had agreed to sublet the room above the timber-yard office to a man he didn't know. The man paid in cash, in person, every January. The room was always cleaner when he left than when he arrived. By the summer of vol 6 Jules had stopped pretending the arrangement was business. He was running cover for Frasier; he and Frasier both knew it. Over the course of the summer he resolved two of Small Wood's harder problems himself — without dispatch, without instruction. He's still in the timber yard at Labor Day. He still doesn't quite like Frasier. He still picks up.

canon_friendship_interludes.title: Mackenzie Finished the Weaving
canon_friendship_interludes.flavor: Mackenzie's loom in the model home's second bedroom held three pieces in progress through the summer: a coverlet for the bed she and Philip don't sleep in together anymore, a wall hanging for the studio in Lacombe she has not used since April, and a small thing the size of a man's hands she would not, when asked, say what was for. By August the wall hanging was finished. The coverlet was nearly. The small thing she finished on Labor Day evening and sent in a brown envelope, without a return address, to the lapels-smoothing gentleman at Table 14. He sent no acknowledgement. Mackenzie did not, by year's end, expect one.

canon_friendship_interludes.title: Mackenzie Left Philip
canon_friendship_interludes.flavor: The fifth Sunday Mackenzie posted on MAINSTREET that she was not, today, going back to Lacombe. By the eighth Sunday she had stopped posting about it. By the end of August the apartment in Lacombe had a sign on the back door in Philip's hand saying NOT FOR FORWARDING. He kept the basil pot watered through October. He did not, in November, water it again. The model home in Harmony Creek had her loom in its second bedroom through the next two summers. Mackenzie still picked up Frasier's calls. Less often. With more between the words.

canon_friendship_interludes.title: The Son Kept the Storefront
canon_friendship_interludes.flavor: By Labor Day weekend the surviving D'Ambrosio son had hosted forty-two Friday dinner services in a row. Sammy at the bar. Hector at the line. Him on the floor. The storefront had three months of insurance paid forward and the beer-and-wine renewal hanging behind the kitchen pass. He had been called away by Frasier three times across the summer; each time he had been back by Saturday lunch. The Friday before Labor Day he stood at the door at 9:42 PM and looked across the dining room and said, to nobody, his father's name. Then he went back to the floor. The room held him.

canon_friendship_interludes.title: The Son Closed the Storefront
canon_friendship_interludes.flavor: August 18. He told Sammy on the Tuesday. Hector on the Wednesday. The other six on the Thursday at 4 PM staff meal. He cried while saying it. The room did not, when he was done, react; they had known since Mardi Gras. The lease ran through the end of October. Sammy bought the brass register. Hector took the eight-burner range to his own kitchen. The son walked out the back door on the last Friday in October at 11:14 PM. He left the door unlocked. He did not, when he reached his car, look back. Frasier's last dispatch had been the one that broke it. Frasier knew. The son knew Frasier knew. By Christmas they were not, anymore, talking.

canon_friendship_interludes.title: John Frank · The Diner Held
canon_friendship_interludes.flavor: Faith II — the dog he named after the first Faith without ever quite calling her that out loud — slept under the counter every night of the summer. John Frank closed the grill at 11 most nights. On the Tuesday before Labor Day a man in lapels sat at booth six for nine minutes. He drank a cup of coffee. He left a hundred-dollar bill under the saucer. John Frank did not look at the bill again until Wednesday morning. By Wednesday afternoon it was in the cathedral's lock box. He did not tell Frasier. He did not need to. Frasier already knew.

canon_friendship_interludes.title: Elicia · The Documentary Landed
canon_friendship_interludes.flavor: Three minutes and forty-six seconds. The choose-your-own-adventure episode dropped on a Friday afternoon in early September with no announcement. By Sunday the cult audience that had been waiting nine years had parsed every frame and posted six different essays. One of the essays — on a quiet forum Elicia would not, in her life, read — identified the lapels in a single background shot. By Monday morning the post had been deleted by its author. Elicia, in the bungalow on Labor Day evening, edited the closing frame by hand and then did not, for the next three months, work on anything else.

canon_friendship_interludes.title: Nicola · Aria at Fourteen
canon_friendship_interludes.flavor: Aria turned fourteen the Saturday before Labor Day. Nicola made the cake at 5 PM. They ate it on the porch at 7. Aria, this birthday, did not ask for the boat. She asked, instead, for a typewriter, and Nicola pretended not to know that the request was an answer to a question her daughter would not, ever, ask out loud. Two of Frasier's dispatches across the summer had taken Nicola out of the house for three days each. Aria had run the house both times. The summer changed Aria — by a half-degree, in the direction she was already going. Nicola noticed at the cake.

canon_friendship_interludes.title: The Diner Grill Went Cold
canon_friendship_interludes.flavor: Two Wednesdays in August the diner did not, by 6 PM, have a grill running. John Frank had been out of town for Frasier on Tuesday and Tuesday and a Friday and the regulars were the first to notice and then a man named Boudreaux Sr who'd been eating at the counter on Wednesdays since 1979 noticed and then John Frank himself noticed on the third Wednesday and lit the grill at 6:14 PM and stood for a long minute with one hand against the rail and said nothing. By September he was, gently, saying no to Frasier's calls. By October he was, gently but more firmly, saying no. Faith II felt the shift before he did and slept against the booth six bench instead of under the counter for the rest of the year.

summer_milestone_interludes.title: Memorial Day
summer_milestone_interludes.flavor: The first day of the summer. Frasier's office in the cathedral was 78°F and humid by 11 AM. The window AC unit had been out since Wednesday. He opened the case files Mackenzie had faxed at midnight. He did not, this morning, open the BBS. The summer's question hung over the desk the way the summer's question had hung over the desk every Memorial Day for nine years. The cathedral hummed around him; the brick walls breathed in their stored heat. By noon he had decided what to do with the first three weeks. By 1 he had started doing it.

summer_milestone_interludes.title: The First BBS Night
summer_milestone_interludes.flavor: The first Sunday. Frasier dialled in at 8:14 PM. The 14.4k handshake took six seconds. The first board was the one with the regulars; the second board was the one with the demons; the third board was the one nobody but Frasier knew about. By 10 PM he had read four threads and answered two DMs. He went to bed at 11. The summer's rhythm had its first night.

summer_milestone_interludes.title: The Sinkhole Anniversary
summer_milestone_interludes.flavor: Mid-July. The sinkhole had been seven years closed. The parish had held a small service at St Jude's at 10 AM. Frasier had not, by intention, attended. He had sent Filly with a wreath from the network; Filly had set it at the side of the marker and left without speaking to anybody. By the time Frasier read the wreath card that evening on the BBS — somebody had photographed it for the parish bulletin — the writing on the card was in a hand Frasier did not, at first, recognise. He recognised it three days later. He didn't tell anyone whose hand it was.

summer_milestone_interludes.title: The Riverboat Anniversary
summer_milestone_interludes.flavor: Late August. Twenty-one years since Dante. The river was high; the levee was where the levee was. Nicola spent the morning at the marker. Her sister came from Houston. They did not, that day, speak much. By evening the parish had a small service at the old riverboat's hull (which is now, technically, a memorial). Frasier sent flowers. He did not send himself. He had not, in twenty-one years, been able to.

summer_milestone_interludes.title: Labor Day Arrived
summer_milestone_interludes.flavor: The hundredth day. The shelf was what it was. The seeds were what they were. Frasier closed the case files on the desk in the order he had opened them. He set them in the cabinet. He closed the cabinet. He sat at the desk for a long minute. He did not, this evening, open the BBS. He drove home the long way through the river country. The summer had been the summer. The book — *Planned Community* — would, in its own time, continue.

cross_cutting_interludes.title: The Cathedral, This Year
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: Twice this summer somebody walked into the warehouse-cathedral who shouldn't have. Once it was a teenage girl who'd been daring herself for six months; she stood in the high bay for eleven minutes, did not touch anything, and walked out as carefully as she had walked in. Once it was a man in a grey suit Frasier did not, at first, recognise. The second time Frasier was in the office. He did not, on either visit, move to interrupt. By September both visitors had, in their own ways, paid the room the kind of respect the room had been waiting for.

cross_cutting_interludes.title: The Demon Roster, Taken Seriously
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: By August Steamboat was posting on RUST_CODE under his own handle. Cicada had begun to ask, in DMs, after the dispatch decisions. Filly had developed, somewhere in late July, a small dry humour that the other demons read and the humans did not. The roster looked less, by the end of the summer, like eight tools and more like eight collaborators with their own ideas about the work. Frasier noticed this in early September. He didn't say so out loud, but on the Friday before Labor Day, he answered Steamboat's DM about the storefront's grease trap with the kind of detail he had not, in twenty years, given a demon.

cross_cutting_interludes.title: The Storefront Kept Going
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: Twelve Friday dinner services across the summer. The lease was paid forward. The beer-and-wine renewal was signed in July. The new Friday menu in late August added a chicken-and-andouille dish Hector had been wanting for two years; it sold out by 8:30 every Friday after that. The room held. The son held. The storefront, this summer, became — quietly, without anybody saying so — the thing it had been trying to become since the family lost the boat.

cross_cutting_interludes.title: The Diner Kept Holding
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: John Frank wiped the same spot of formica seven hundred and seventy-five times this summer. Faith II slept under the counter. The fluorescent above booth six ticked once every two seconds, all summer, without ever once tipping the room over. Two threshold visits — both quiet, both resolved by John Frank himself without dispatch — landed in the diner's small lore-tokens column. The cards on the back wall did not, this summer, claim anyone. The diner ended the summer the same diner it had been.

cross_cutting_interludes.title: The Tower Was Dim Most Weeks
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: Dean's tower in Small Wood was dim for thirteen of the summer's fifteen weeks. Two weeks it warmed; once it went bright; never, this summer, did it go white. The anomalies were small enough that the player who wasn't watching for them might have missed them entirely. Dean was, in this particular summer, busy with something else. Frasier noted this. He did not — could not — say what.

cross_cutting_interludes.title: An Agent at the Tower
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: Frasier sent an agent at the tower on a date he later refused to write down. The agent did not return. Frasier removed the agent's name from the dispatch rotation on the Monday after, and from the BBS handle list on the Tuesday, and from the dossier wall in the office on the Wednesday. The wall has, since 1997, a small unfilled rectangle where the dossier used to be. None of the other agents asked. None of them ever did.

cross_cutting_interludes.title: The Turned Demon Became Small-Wood-Shaped
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: By late August she was walking the north road at 6:14 AM without being asked. Mrs. Delacroix said good morning to her at the mailbox and she said good morning back. Mr. Beauchamp tipped his hat once and she nodded. The dog at the corner of Ninth stopped barking at her the second week of July and never resumed. By Labor Day she was — quietly, without anyone including her having named the thing — part of the region's daily texture. Husk noted this in a note to himself on a legal pad on the safehouse kitchen table; he wrote 'she is Small-Wood-shaped now' and then, after a minute, drew a line through the word 'now.'

cross_cutting_interludes.title: The Infiltration Paid
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: She came back through the safehouse at 4 AM Saturday four times across the summer. Each visit she named three of Frasier's forward-scouts, drew the parish-road route their handler ran, and slept until noon. Husk cooked eggs on the two visits he was there for; on the two he wasn't, the eggs were there when she woke up, in a covered pan on the stove that stayed warm. Nobody wrote it down. The intel was walking-around intel, kept in Husk's memory and Frasier's. By Labor Day the resistance had, without any one of them saying it out loud, closed three of Frasier's roads without having to spend cover to do it.

cross_cutting_interludes.title: The Becoming Took
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: She read every one of Elicia's newsletters in the safehouse over the course of eleven days. She read the Sears catalog Husk brought her from the mailbox. She learned what a cul-de-sac was; she learned the difference between the parish road and the state highway; she learned which of the neighbours had a dog and which had a cat and which had, in the case of Mrs. Delacroix, a small parrot named Sœur. On the twenty-second day she walked to the mailbox by herself. On the twenty-third she asked Husk for a book from town. By Labor Day the safehouse had, without anybody using the word, become her house. Husk moved his coat to the second peg.

cross_cutting_interludes.title: The Basement Was Read
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: Frasier's sysop-log for THE_BASEMENT recorded fewer visits than any other hidden board and more re-reads per visit than any of the others combined. The player who found the room went back to it, again and again, quietly, on Sunday nights. The demons never posted more than they were meant to. The player read what they read and closed the modem. The room did what the room was for.

cross_cutting_interludes.title: The Roster Stayed Quiet
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: Not one of the eight demons crossed into hungry across the full summer. Nobody spilled a signature. Nobody posted a reply outside the basement. Husk kept the coat on inside the safehouse and never once had to close a door louder than it needed to be closed. Frasier noticed this on the Friday before Labor Day and, for the first time all year, allowed himself the two-fingers-of-bourbon he had been withholding. He did not, at any point, thank the player. He never would.

cross_cutting_interludes.title: The Press Softened
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: Wednesday's B6 was the cathedral fundraiser piece, again, and the Wednesday after that was a story about the parish garden club, and the Wednesday after that was a photograph of Sister Beatrice at the parish steps. Roy Castellan's timber-yard file was in the drawer by mid-July and did not come out again. Elicia's ledger of favours had, by Labor Day, a small column marked 'roy' with three tick marks against it. She had not, this summer, called any of them in.

cross_cutting_interludes.title: The Safehouse Held the Summer
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: The safehouse porch off the back road held two guests this summer where in past summers it had held one. Husk cooked more often than he had in previous years. Filly came in three times and stayed the third time for a full six days. The porch light was off past 11 PM every one of those nights. The road did not carry a car past midnight for the entire window. The porch, for a summer that could have made it a checkpoint, remained a porch.

cross_cutting_interludes.title: Graustark Held Through Bertha
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: The storm turned north of the keys and made land in the panhandle at 3 AM Saturday. The cathedral basement was the inland relay for eighteen hours. STEEPLE was on the bell-buoy in Mobile; WIRE_MOTHER was on the panhandle line; JF was on the boiler; T. was at the storefront alone. The line stayed up. At 4:14 AM Saturday the desk-holder stood up because he had gone to check the boiler gauge, and Frasier's tally sheet for the summer recorded, in the storm-window column, the words 'held clean.' He underlined them once.

cross_cutting_interludes.title: The Roster Learned to Share a Region
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: Five times across the summer two demons worked the same region on the same weekend. Steamboat and Weir at the parish dock at 4 AM · Filly and Husk on the state-line road, one carrying and one carrying the road behind her · Moth and the porch light at the house Vagrant had walked past the night before. The roster, at the start of the summer, had been eight tools stored on separate shelves. By September it was a room of collaborators who knew when the other one was in the region. Frasier read the log at Labor Day and closed it without making a note.

cross_cutting_interludes.title: The Humans Worked in Pairs
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: JF at the boiler while the surviving son ran the storefront's front · Nicola in the storefront's kitchen while Aria did her homework at booth 2 · Elicia and Mackenzie on the Wednesday alignment that softened the newsletter · Jules and the surviving son on the '94 protocol for the room above the yard. Four times across the summer the network moved as two hands where in past summers it had moved as one. The parish read it as normal-Sunday every time. Frasier, watching from the office, allowed the corners of his mouth to lift once. He did not repeat the expression.

cross_cutting_interludes.title: The Basement Did the Deeper Work
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: Three times across the summer the player took a Sunday night to spend on THE_BASEMENT and one of those Sundays the demons who were hungry came back Monday morning steady. The rite is not written down. It is not spoken of on the other boards. It is what THE_BASEMENT is for on the Sundays when the burn is not the problem. Frasier's sysop-log records the sessions as fifty-minute visits. It does not record what was read. That is between the reader and the room.

cross_cutting_interludes.title: The Humans Took Their Quiet Weeks
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: Four times across the summer the loom stayed threaded and Mackenzie sat at it. Four times the diner's Wednesday shift ran with John Frank at the grill and only the grill. Four times Nicola opened the storefront at 5 AM and closed it at 9 without a dispatch stacked on top. The obligation ledger came down by one, then again, then again. The summer was not a summer of no cost. It was, though, a summer of cost paid in weeks that the humans got to keep.

cross_cutting_interludes.title: Cover Held in All Three Regions
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: Small Wood at 5, Graustark at 6, Harmony Creek at 5. Not one of the three dropped below the working threshold at any point in the summer. Mrs. Delacroix vetoed the second-camera motion for the twelfth time in July. Roy Castellan's Gazette softened by mid-August. Jules's dirt road held. The three regions ended the summer in the shape the summer had needed them to end in. Frasier's ledger, on the last page for 1996, has a single line — 'three good regions, still there.'

cross_cutting_interludes.title: The Summer Broke a Region
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: One of the three regions ended the summer with cover at zero. Frasier read the tally sheet for it once at 9 PM on Labor Day and once at 6 AM Tuesday morning. Both times he sat with it for a long minute before turning the page. The region is still there. The people in it are still there. But the region will need three summers to get back to where it was in June. Frasier will spend some of those summers on it. He will not, on any of them, say so out loud.

cross_cutting_interludes.title: Attention Never Crested
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: The Genetec vans that showed up on the parish road in July had, by August, moved on. The seventh-camera item on the HOA agenda never landed. Roy Castellan's timber-yard file stayed in the drawer. The neighborhood watch counted the same fifty-nine phone lines it had counted in May. Attention, across all three regions, never once crested three at any point in the summer. The summer was, in the technical language of the ledger, quiet.

cross_cutting_interludes.title: The Gauntlet Kept Being Played
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: In the office at 11 PM on the nights when the dispatch board was quiet, Frasier played through the cards. He beat three of them across the summer. He kept the pen in the top-left drawer with the finished cards; the cards themselves went back into the deck. The other agents did not know he did this. Aria, walking past the office at 6 AM once on her way to the storefront, saw the pen on the desk and did not, on the way out, mention it. The gauntlet was, this summer, Frasier's own small night-time practice.

cross_cutting_interludes.title: The Gauntlet Was a Second Summer
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: Ten arcana closed by Labor Day. The pen moved from drawer to desk and back to drawer more nights than not. Frasier played through cards he had not played in three years. Some of them landed differently in the summer of Aria's rebind; the Death card in particular, this year, read as an arithmetic he could no longer perform without carrying a two he had not known was there. The gauntlet is not a second job. It is, though, a second summer running in parallel to the first, and the player who noticed this ran both of them.

cross_cutting_interludes.title: The Scrapbook Was Full
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: Twenty lore tokens across the twenty-two arcana by early September. The scrapbook, at the start of the summer, had been a room of question marks. By Labor Day it was a room of names. The Lacombe sisters. Mrs Delacroix. Jules. Fr Elias Landry. The teenage girl who stood in the warehouse-cathedral for eleven minutes. The scrapbook is not the map of Frasier's world. It is the map of what the player found while walking through it.

cross_cutting_interludes.title: The Crossover Fired
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: Twice this summer a choice on the dispatch board opened a card in the gauntlet that had not been available before. The kid at the cathedral in July; the demon Frasier had trained, quietly committed in August. The two games are, in Frasier's ledger, one game. The player who found this made the connection on their own terms. The card unlocked itself the way cards unlock themselves in a life: because you did the thing the card needed you to do.

cross_cutting_interludes.title: The Magician and Death Both Landed
cross_cutting_interludes.flavor: Two cards Frasier has spent his life inside: the Magician (his own warehouse-cathedral, the parish's small acts of intercession) and Death (the arithmetic of who leaves and how). The player closed them both this summer. Neither card was easy; both were, in their different ways, honest. Frasier read the finished-cards page in his notebook on the Sunday of Labor Day weekend. He did not, this evening, tally the cards he had not yet finished. The two that had landed were enough for the page.

aria_summer_w11_interludes.title: The Office Held the Room
aria_summer_w11_interludes.flavor: Tuesday, August 6, 1996. The cathedral office between 6:08 and 9:42 PM. Mom in the desk chair. Nicola at the small table. Aria standing at the window. JF at the door. WIRE_MOTHER on the speakerphone, reading the names from her kitchen in Lubbock at a pace that let the room keep up. The cover Frasier spent in Graustark this week was the cover the office was built with — it had been there for the kind of evening this evening was. Aria did not flinch. The third did not come. The binding holds in a way the 1981 binding did not hold, because the 1981 binding was written for a foal that had not yet asked her own questions, and the 1996 binding has Aria's name on it in Aria's own hand.

aria_summer_w11_interludes.title: The Drive Home Was Quiet
aria_summer_w11_interludes.flavor: After the storm-watch weekend in late August, Nicola drove Aria home from the cathedral office at 11:48 PM. The relay had held; the cathedral basement line had been the line the gulf coast called when the bell-buoy in Mobile lost its bracket. Aria had sat at the office desk through all of it, keeping a list of who had called in from where. On the drive home she asked her mother whether the third had been in the office during the storm. Nicola said no. Aria nodded. She had not been afraid; she had been at work. The work was the work. The binding had held precisely because she had been at the desk, doing it.

aria_summer_w11_interludes.title: The Box on the High Shelf
aria_summer_w11_interludes.flavor: After the August 6 meeting Aria put the third's drawn shape into a wooden box JF had built for her in 1994 — a box meant, originally, for the seashells she had collected the summer before, when Pop's mother died. She put the box on the high shelf behind the cathedral office door. Nicola does not open the box. Aria opens the box. The box is, this summer and the next several summers, the room Aria holds. The circle is the room around the room. WIRE_MOTHER calls from Lubbock on the fourth Sunday of each month and asks Aria how the box is. Aria says the box is fine. The third's voice, that summer, did not get quieter — but Aria learned to write it down before it carried.

aria_summer_w11_interludes.title: What Aria Held
aria_summer_w11_interludes.flavor: The burn from the W11 choice landed in W13. The third's voice — louder than Aria had expected, louder than Nicola had expected, louder than Frasier had hoped — carried out of the box one Sunday evening when Nicola was at the storefront. Aria did not call for help. She walked from the back room to the kitchen, picked up the wooden spoon Mom kept by the stove, and put it across the threshold of her bedroom door. The third's voice did not cross. She put the spoon back. She wrote down what she had heard. She put the page in the box. The next morning she told Nicola, plainly, what she had done. Nicola did not argue with her. The page is in the box.

aria_summer_w11_interludes.title: The Bungalow Kept Aria
aria_summer_w11_interludes.flavor: Elicia put Aria in the back room of the bungalow on the evening of August 6. The back room has the window that looks onto the cottonwood, the same cottonwood Elicia has photographed every August since 1990 and uploaded to the art board as ASCII for the last three Augusts. The third did not know the way to the bungalow because, as Elicia explained to Aria on the first morning at breakfast, the bungalow is where the names go that don't fit in the cathedral. Aria nodded and wrote it down. By the end of August Aria was making her own list — not of the third, but of the third's friends. The ones, she said, who were going to come looking.

aria_summer_w11_interludes.title: Labor Day at the Bungalow
aria_summer_w11_interludes.flavor: Aria did not come to the Labor Day cookout at the storefront. Elicia drove down for the afternoon and stayed through dinner; she brought a print of the cottonwood and a folder of photographs from Aria's August. The folder had eleven photographs. The eleventh was the back of Aria's head at the bungalow's kitchen table, leaning over her notebook. Mom looked at the photograph for a long time. She did not say anything about it. She put the photograph in the kitchen drawer at the storefront where she keeps the photographs of the family she does not show the family. Nicola called Aria from the storefront's payphone at 7:48 PM and said she was sorry it had taken her so long to call. Aria said she had been waiting for the named hour.
